texts.full_text: "very rewarding experience" for all involved according to organiser Rene Wkyes. "It's about bringing everyone together and forming strong partnerships so that we are getting maximum benefit to communities and people," Ms Wkyes, a Training Manager with Murdi Paaki Regional Enterprise Corporation, explained. "I think from this forum we will have a strong network of women from across the region who will continue to drive the women's gathering. "We hope that it is a process that they are able to continue to communicate and provide relevant information up to government bodies and service providers working across the regions as to what is required and what is needed," Ms Wkyes continued. "One thing that has come out of the forum is that not everyone was aware of all the services that are on offer," Ms Wkyes said. "There's been a good flow of information and it has been very rewarding to see how well everyone has come together to achieve positive outcomes. "If we can develop some strong partnerships and the linkages for an on-going network for the women that will mean the forum has been a success," Ms Wkyes added. The event, which brought together over 60 Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander women from across the Central West and surrounding regions, featured workshops on cultural preservation, mental health advocacy, and economic empowerment. Participants also engaged in storytelling circles and skill-sharing sessions, fostering connections that transcended community boundaries. A key focus of the gathering was addressing gaps in service accessibility, with attendees collaborating to draft a community needs assessment report. This document, Ms. Wkyes noted, will be presented to local councils and state government agencies to inform policy decisions. "It’s about amplifying our voices and ensuring our priorities—like better access to education, healthcare, and land rights—are at the forefront of discussions," she said. texts.full_text: is served by Via Rail's Jasper – Prince Rupert train aCedarvale RailwayStation is an integral part of British Columbia’s railway history. Constructed in the early 20th century around 1914 to support log exports from nearby forests and connect remote areas like Horseshoe Lake Provincial Park with major cities, it was once a vital stop on Via Rail's Jasper–Prince Rupert route. The station served passengers traveling between Vancouver Island and Northern BC until its closure in the late mid-20th century. Today, while no passenger trains operate through Cedarvale anymore due to declining railway use and improved road networks, remnants of this historical site remain as a testament to our past logging industry's significance and rail history. texts.full_text: uses it to encrypt a symmetrical key which they will use throughout the rest of the conversation. This is done because symmetrical cryptology is quicker than asymmetrical. Afterwards, when the server receives the encrypted symmetrical key, the server uses its Private Key to decrypt it and then use it for the rest of the conversation. At this point, everything looks plain and easy, however a priori one doesn't have every agent Public Key. So, in order to establish a secure communication with someone, they need to exchange Public Keys first; and this is where PKC weakness lies. How would I know that the Public Key given to me is effectively from who I want to communicate and is not from a Man-in-the-Middle (MITM)? This is the problem that Public Key Infrastructure (PKI) solves. Public Key Infrastructure (PKI) consists of adding another person to the conversation. This other agent is the Certificate Authority (CA), who acts as a neutral judge which is trusted by the two other participants of the conversation. This CA (who also has his Public and Private Key) is the one that signs the Public Key of any PKI user after certifying that they are the person they claim to be. Consequently, whenever an agent of the PKI receive a Public Key, they verify if it has been signed by one of his trusted CAs. Before establishing a SSL/TLS connection, the client needs to be sure that the received certificate is valid. In order to do it the client verifies not only the authenticity of its public key but also other metadata associated with it (to understand this is important to know the contents of a typical digital certificate): The signature verifies. texts.full_text: Martin Bronstein Martin Bronstein (born 1935) is a British-Canadian actor, writer, columnist, broadcaster and journalist. Early life and education Bronstein was born in London, England. Career Bronstein moved to Canada in 1959 and worked as a copywriter, journalist and comedy writer. He also worked for the Canadian Broadcasting Corporation interviewing a series of musicians, including Oscar Peterson, Dave Brubeck, Bob Dylan, Dizzy Gillespie, Sir Malcolm Sargent, Nina Simonand many others. In 1966 he wrote his first book "The Nightjar". In the late '60s and early-to-mid-70's Bronstein hosted a radio show on CFRB in Toronto. The program was titled: Tons of Fun with MartinBronstein, it lasted for four years from October to December. In 1972 he appeared as an actor at London’s Royal Court Theatre alongside Simon Rae and Paul Kaye (the latter being the first film appearance). In "Gilbert's Unmarried Widow" Bronsteins role was 'Pepsi'. Bronstein wrote two books about his childhood memories of life in a Jewish community, namely: “A Portrait on the Folding Screen” - and later another book with similar themes called: The Glass Ballerina. In "Gilbert's Unmarried Widow" Bronsteins role was 'Pepsi'. texts.full_text: e quality and handling of their latest motorcycle rubber, Michelin stepped outside of the box and combined two seemingly mutually exclusive construction methods: bias and radial ply. The basic premise is simple: achieve the overall structural stiffness and load-bearing capabilities of a bias ply,combined with reduced rolling resistance like that seen in low-profile radius-ply tires. To accomplish this harmonious blend between two construction styles without resorting to compromise on either aspect—which would result from the usual "either/or" dichotomy of their chosen methodologies, Michelin utilized an advanced manufacturing technique called hybridization. This process entails taking specific tire segments and applying a bias-ply build within those sections while retaining radial ply technology in other areas. For instance: tires may have different designs depending on where they are mounted—radial for the front to ensure better maneuverability (often required by motorcycle enthusiasts who value precise handling), whereas biased plies might be found at critical points like tire sidewalls, which take significant pressure during cornering. Hybridization has been a secretive practice in certain industries; it's usually applied when there is an unmet demand for more specialized products that require unique features not typically seen with standard designs. We looked to buy new as I know very well that buying old comes with problems, after looking at different websites on what may be best suited for us we narrowed down three vehicles which ticked all of them boxes:- VW Passat Ford Mondeo (03/51 Plate) Renault Megane 2.7dci Scenic LX(4 door)(F9 reg.) Our R8’s back seat did fold right-down so this meant that the rear part where I could stand was very tight, but with our youngest and her cot it really wasn’t enough room to keep what we needed in there let alone us travelling along. Our passenger side didn't move all of which would make traveling quite a bit more uncomfortable than being able just shift over an inch or so from one seat-to-the-next as was the case for my partner on long journey’s when she used our other Renault R16. So in closing, we decided that out these three vehicles listed above it had to be either VW Passat which I would of purchased but they were only coming up with 03 plates or an older Mondeo; although still available today this didn’t appeal so much because you get caught paying £245 tax if its over four years old! Also being on my own driving a five-seater isn't exactly economical, So leaving us the final contender of which has proven to be far more than enough... texts.full_text: of the Virgin __NOTOC__ The Master of the Life of the Virgin, in German the Meister des Marienlebens, (working ca. 1463 — ca. 1490), is the pseudonym given to a late Gothic German painter working in Cologne. He can also be known as the Master of Wilten, or Johann van Duyren, an identification not universally accepted. His workshop is identified by his masterwork, a series of eight depictions of conventional scenes from the Life of the Virgin Mary, painted for the Ursulakirche, Cologne; seven are in the Alte Pinakothek, Munich, and one in the National Gallery, London, who also have the four outer panels from an altarpiece from Werden, whose central panel is lost, but is known from a drawing. Other works are in the Prado, Madrid, the Louvre, Paris, and the Metropolitan Museum of Art, New York. His style is characterized by a strong influence from the painting of Rogier van der Weyden and a clear, bright, and precise technique, which may have influenced the development of the painting of the young Albrecht Dürer.
